reply_text: @marknewbold2583 No, as a European I happen to know that children over here, as well, often only come in contact with Frankenstein through pop-culture: Halloween masks, toys, characters based on Frankenstein's monster named "Frank" in tv-shows and video games, etc. So of course they come to believe that "Frankenstein" is the name of the monster, and the corporations that churn out the masks and toys and video games don't care about informing them otherwise. It also doesn't help that the book itself is often considered "too mature" for young children to read/have read to themselves, so the misunderstanding persists until the book ends up as school or course literature.

reply_text: @DvanderPluijm I agree. While yes, Victor left him alone after being scared of what he did, the Creation went out and people were horrified by him. Even when he showed kindness to the people in the cottage and left them wood, when they saw him, they ran him out. I like to see it as a new baby coming into a world that is nothing but cruel to them. I don’t think Victor is the monster but I don’t think the Creation is either. I think Victor had a right to be scared, to think he went too far, to be mad that his Creation hurt people Victor loved and want revenge, but he created this “Monster” and chose to throw it out into the world due to his fear and didn’t help it feel welcomed or loved. I also think that the Creation is alone, and been shown no kindness, didn’t ask to be brought here, loves his creator and wants answers from him, wants someone to accept him and lashes out in ways that inexcusable. I love the complexity of the story. Where you feel shock and anger and heartache for both characters from the story. I think the true monster is society, how we turn away from people, how we treat others who are different.

video_title: Frankenstein | Guillermo del Toro | Official Teaser | Netflix
reply_text: ​​​@charlitaylor3623 Oh, I understand. When the Monster talks about his pain and loneliness, he's not lying; he really, really feels bad. When he says to himself: "Miserable, unhappy wretch!", "Was I, then, a monster, a blot upon the earth, from which all men fled and whom all men disowned? I cannot describe to you the agony that these reflections inflicted upon me; I tried to dispel them, but sorrow only increased with knowledge" - he's honest. The Monster's main problem is his perception of the world and himself in it. He didn't consider himself to be in any way connected, not just to people(even morally), but to all living beings and even to nature (I suppose reading Paradise Lost, where everything beautiful is created for people, didn't help the Monster accept himself in the world at all).  When he asked for a "Companion," he said, "My vices are the children of a forced solitude that I abhor, and my virtues will necessarily arise when I live in communion with an equal. I shall feel the affections of a sensitive being and become linked to the chain of existence and events from which I am now excluded" - which well illustrates the Monster's worldview. But he is also poor at reflecting on his actions and circumstances, even when he mentions his crimes in the final monologue - he does so in the context of the suffering his actions have caused him. And the Monster too often generalized ALL PEOPLE. He interpreted his good deeds as a great blessing, his crimes as forced circumstances, and protecting people (Felix protects his father, sister and wife) from the Monster (the threat he potentially poses) - as betrayal and rejection. The moment in the finale when the Мonster accuses all of humanity, but cites only two episodes;  and then explains to Walton, what Walton (and other people) supposedly think, that is, the Monster projects his thoughts onto others: “Still I desired love and fellowship, and I was still spurned. Was there no injustice in this? Am I to be thought the only criminal, when all humankind sinned against me? Why do you not hate Felix, who drove his friend from his door with contumely? Why do you not execrate the rustic who sought to destroy the savior of his child? Nay, these are virtuous and immaculate beings! I, the miserable and the abandoned, am an abortion, to be spurned at, and kicked, and trampled on. Even now my blood boils at the recollection of this injustice.” In fact, the Monster was most often rejected by the very people he imagined in his head.
